title: String Equality
description: Ashish has two strings $$$a$$$ and $$$b$$$, each of length $$$n$$$, and an integer $$$k$$$. The strings only contain lowercase English letters. He wants to convert string $$$a$$$ into string $$$b$$$ by performing some (possibly zero) operations on $$$a$$$. In one move, he can either choose an index $$$i$$$ ($$$1 \leq i\leq n-1$$$) and swap $$$a_i$$$ and $$$a_{i+1}$$$, or choose an index $$$i$$$ ($$$1 \leq i \leq n-k+1$$$) and if $$$a_i, a_{i+1}, \ldots, a_{i+k-1}$$$ are all equal to some character $$$c$$$ ($$$c \neq$$$ ' z '), replace each one with the next character $$$(c+1)$$$, that is, ' a ' is replaced by ' b ', ' b ' is replaced by ' c ' and so on. Note that he can perform any number of operations, and the operations can only be performed on string $$$a$$$. Help Ashish determine if it is possible to convert string $$$a$$$ into $$$b$$$ after performing some (possibly zero) operations on it.
input_spec: The first line contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \leq t \leq 10^5$$$) — the number of test cases. The description of each test case is as follows. The first line of each test case contains two integers $$$n$$$ ($$$2 \leq n \leq 10^6$$$) and $$$k$$$ ($$$1 \leq k \leq n$$$). The second line of each test case contains the string $$$a$$$ of length $$$n$$$ consisting of lowercase English letters. The third line of each test case contains the string $$$b$$$ of length $$$n$$$ consisting of lowercase English letters. It is guaranteed that the sum of values $$$n$$$ among all test cases does not exceed $$$10^6$$$.
output_spec: For each test case, print " Yes " if Ashish can convert $$$a$$$ into $$$b$$$ after some moves, else print " No ". You may print the letters of the answer in any case (upper or lower).

examples.input:
4
3 3
abc
bcd
4 2
abba
azza
2 1
zz
aa
6 2
aaabba
ddddcc

routes.name: Frequency sweep with carry
routes.description: Since unlimited adjacent swaps make position irrelevant, reduce the problem to character frequencies. Process letters from 'a' to 'z', carrying excess counts forward in multiples of k.

observations.title: Unlimited swaps make positions irrelevant
observations.description: Adjacent swaps can be applied any number of times, which means we can rearrange the string into any permutation. Therefore the positions of characters do not matter at all -- only the frequency of each letter matters.

hints.nudge: What can you achieve with unlimited adjacent swaps? Think about what sorting algorithms do.
hints.question: If you can swap any two adjacent elements as many times as you want, can you always reach any permutation of the string? What does that simplify?
hints.formal: Repeated adjacent swaps are equivalent to bubble sort, so any permutation is reachable. The problem reduces to: can we transform the character frequency array of a into that of b using the increment operation?

observations.title: Characters can only increase, never decrease
observations.description: The increment operation replaces k copies of character c with k copies of c+1. There is no operation to decrease a character. This means we can only move character counts upward in the alphabet, never downward.

hints.nudge: Look at both operations carefully. Is there any way to turn a 'b' back into an 'a'?
hints.question: If string a has more 'z's than string b needs, can you ever get rid of the excess? What does this imply about the direction characters can flow?
hints.formal: The only way to change character values is the increment operation, which strictly increases them. So if at any letter we have fewer characters in a than b requires, and there is no surplus from earlier letters to promote, it is impossible.

observations.title: Excess characters must come in multiples of k
observations.description: The increment operation converts exactly k copies of a letter into k copies of the next letter. This means any surplus of a letter that we want to promote must be a multiple of k -- we cannot promote a partial group.

hints.nudge: How many characters does the increment operation affect at once? What constraint does that place on leftovers?
hints.question: If you have 5 extra 'a's and k = 3, can you promote all 5 of them to 'b'? What happens to the remainder?
hints.formal: Since each increment operation converts exactly k characters, the difference (count_a[c] - count_b[c]) at each letter, after accounting for carry from previous letters, must be a non-negative multiple of k. If it is negative or not divisible by k, output NO.

observations.title: Sweep from 'a' to 'z' carrying surplus forward
observations.description: Process letters in alphabetical order. At each letter c, compute the surplus: have[c] - need[c]. If the surplus is negative or not divisible by k, the answer is NO. Otherwise, carry the surplus forward by adding it to have[c+1], since promoting k copies of c produces k copies of c+1.

hints.nudge: If you process the smallest letter first and handle its excess, where does that excess go?
hints.question: After you decide to promote the surplus of letter c, which letter's count increases? How would you check all 26 letters systematically?
hints.formal: For c = 'a' to 'z': compute diff = have[c] - need[c]. If diff < 0 or diff % k != 0, answer is NO. Otherwise set have[c+1] += diff. If all 26 letters pass, answer is YES.
